nodes.label: 1.1.3 Reading and drawing graphs, interpreting tables
1.2.2 Demand factors, shifts vs movements along the curve
1.3.2 Cost structures: TK, VK, CK, GTK, GVK, GCK
1.4.2 Shifts in supply and demand, new equilibria
1.4.4 Profit maximisation: the MO = MK rule
2.1.2 Elastic vs inelastic: the connection to omzet (revenue)
2.2.2 Producer surplus, total surplus, and the link to marginal cost
2.3.1 Taxes: the wedge and the new equilibrium
2.3.2 Taxes: burden sharing, afwenteling and welfare loss
3.1.2 Profit maximisation: MO = MK at volkomen concurrentie
3.2.2 MO at monopoly: understanding the declining MO curve
3.2.3 Profit maximisation at monopoly: full calculation
3.3.1 Welfare effects of monopoly: surplus and Harberger triangle
4.1.1 Labor demand: derived demand and marginal productivity
4.2.2 Minimum wage: welfare effects on the labor market
4.3.2 Gains from trade, terms of trade
4.4.3 Real vs nominal: integration and application
1.1.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Draw a (P,Q) graph from a table with the economist's axis convention (P vertical, Q horizontal) (A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 1.1.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Read values from a (P,Q) graph; interpolate between data points (A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 1.2.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Classify a normal vs inferior good response to an income change (concept-level, prerequisite for D11 inkomenselasticiteit)

nodes.label: 1.2.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Horizontal sum: aggregate individual demand tables into collective demand (A-domain candidate, mirror of A31 for aanbod)

nodes.label: 1.2.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Algebraic horizontal sum of linear demand functions (A-domain candidate, Q-at-equal-P)

nodes.label: 1.2.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Recognise the kink in collective demand when one consumer exits (concept-level)

nodes.label: 1.3.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Draw an upward-sloping supply curve with correct economist axes (A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 1.3.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute GVK = TVK/Q and GCK = TCK/Q (A-domain candidates; A14 covers GTK but not GVK/GCK separately)

nodes.label: 1.3.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Explain why GCK falls as Q rises (spreading fixed costs) (concept-level, foundation for long-run equilibrium in §3.1.3)

nodes.label: 1.3.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Observe GO = P at a constant price (concept-level, a single-step recognition)

nodes.label: 1.4.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Determine surplus (aanbodoverschot) vs shortage (vraagoverschot) at a non-equilibrium price and calculate its size (A-domain candidate, prerequisite for government price interventions in §2.4)

nodes.label: 1.4.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Reason about simultaneous supply+demand shifts: determinate Q-direction but ambiguous P-direction (concept-level, diagnostic checkpoint)

nodes.label: 1.4.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute MK and MO from a table by taking differences (table-method, precedes A11/A12/A13 differentiation — the pre-calculus foundation sequence must NOT use calculus; a table-only variant should be its own unit)

nodes.label: 1.4.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Observe MK constant for linear TK vs increasing for quadratic TK (concept-level pattern recognition)

nodes.label: 1.4.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Observe MO = P when price is constant (concept-level)

nodes.label: 1.4.4 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Approximate MK from TK = a + bQ² as 2bQ without formal differentiation (early pre-calculus bridge; a distinct unit from A11 'Afgeleide bepalen')

nodes.label: 1.4.4 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Verify a profit optimum by checking winst at Q−1, Q, Q+1 (A-domain candidate, good-habits-building step)

nodes.label: 2.1.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Classify demand as elastic vs inelastic from |Ev| against the 1-threshold (concept-level, tight pairing with A15)

nodes.label: 2.1.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Explain intuitively why substitute-rich or luxury goods are more elastic (concept-level; necessity-vs-luxury, substitute availability)

nodes.label: 2.1.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: State and apply the Ev↔omzet-direction rule to advise a pricing move (concept-level, the business-relevance payoff of §2.1.1)

nodes.label: 2.1.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Classify normal vs inferior from Ei sign; luxury vs necessity from |Ei| vs 1 (concept-level, pairs with A17)

nodes.label: 2.1.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Read a multi-variable demand function Qx(Px, Py, Y) and evaluate at a point (A-domain candidate, ceteris paribus in action — the Part-C novel skill)

nodes.label: 2.1.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Predict Qd change from elasticity × percentage change in driver (A-domain candidate; inverts the measurement formula)

nodes.label: 2.2.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Verbal: interpret CS as aggregate willingness-to-pay minus paid-price (concept-level, welfare-framing)

nodes.label: 2.2.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Reinterpret the supply curve as the marginal cost curve (concept-level; critical for monopoly welfare in §3.3.1)

nodes.label: 2.2.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compute total surplus TS = CS + PS and recognise it as the competitive-equilibrium welfare metric (concept-level)

nodes.label: 2.2.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Determine the short-side rule (transactions at a forced price = min(Qd, Qs)) and name which side rations (concept-level + application)

nodes.label: 2.2.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Give a verbal Pareto-efficiency definition and argue why competitive equilibrium satisfies it (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 2.3.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Distinguish consumer price Pc vs producer price Pp and the tax wedge = Pc − Pp (concept-level + grafisch)

nodes.label: 2.3.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute afwentelingspercentage = (Pc − P*) / tax × 100 and interpret it (A-domain candidate; the consumer-burden metric)

nodes.label: 2.3.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Apply the surplus-accounting identity: old TS = new CS + new PS + tax revenue + DWL (concept-level, completeness check)

nodes.label: 2.3.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Explain that subsidies cause DWL via overproduction beyond the Pareto-efficient quantity (concept-level, counterintuitive to students)

nodes.label: 2.3.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compute government subsidy expenditure = subsidy × Q_new (reken skill, A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 2.4.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Structure a beoordeel-answer: who wins, who loses under a max price — renters-with-a-unit vs renters-without (concept-level + verbal skill)

nodes.label: 2.4.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute the cost to the government of buying up the aanbodoverschot = (Qs − Qd) × Pmin (reken skill, A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 2.4.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Evaluate distributional effect: who gains (producers), who pays (consumers + taxpayers) (concept-level + verbal)

nodes.label: 2.4.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Invert the demand function to read price from a given quota quantity (A-domain candidate; bridges to §3.2 monopoly mechanics)

nodes.label: 2.4.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compare welfare effects of quota vs minimum price vs tax for the same producer-price target (concept-level + analytical; exam-integration skill)

nodes.label: 2.4.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Explain why producers can prefer a quota to free-market output (concept-level, income-certainty argument)

nodes.label: 3.1.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Name the four characteristics of volkomen concurrentie and recognise them in a concrete market (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.1.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Explain why an individual firm in volkomen concurrentie is a hoeveelheidsaanpasser / price taker (concept-level)

nodes.label: 3.1.1 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Show P = GO = MO for the individual firm (grafisch + concept; the horizontal price line representation)

nodes.label: 3.1.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Apply the power rule to differentiate aQ² + bQ + c, producing 2aQ + b (A-domain candidate; A11 covers 'Afgeleide bepalen' generally but not this specific TK→MK recipe)

nodes.label: 3.1.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: State MO = P as a special case for a price-taker firm (concept-level, tight pair with §3.1.1)

nodes.label: 3.1.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Herleid GTK uit TK door term-voor-term delen door Q (A-domain candidate; the 500/Q-term is the didactic bottleneck explicitly scaffolded into §3.1.2)

nodes.label: 3.1.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Argue that entry erodes positive profit and exit eliminates losses, so long-run profit = 0 in volkomen concurrentie (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.1.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Recognise the long-run equilibrium geometric condition P = min GTK = MK and explain why (concept-level + grafisch)

nodes.label: 3.2.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Name the typical causes of monopoly — legal, natural, patent — with an example each (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.2.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Contrast prijszetter vs hoeveelheidsaanpasser and link to the slope of the firm-level demand curve (concept-level)

nodes.label: 3.2.1 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Judge real-world market power claims against the four structural characteristics (concept-level + verbal analysis; the 'is Apple really a monopolist?' style question)

nodes.label: 3.2.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Explain why MO < P for a monopolist — the 'price-cut-on-all-units' argument (concept-level, the non-obvious insight of this paragraph)

nodes.label: 3.2.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Observe that the MO curve has twice the slope of a linear demand curve (concept-level + grafisch; pattern recognition before calculus)

nodes.label: 3.2.2 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Build TO = P×Q from a linear P(Q) and expand into aQ + bQ² form (A-domain candidate; step-0 of the §3.2.3 procedure)

nodes.label: 3.2.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Execute the full 6-step monopoly procedure end-to-end (A35 is registered as 'Max. winst monopolist'; this procedure-execution skill is what it should canonically encode — verify A35's procedure field covers it)

nodes.label: 3.2.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Explain why charging above P* reduces profit — the demand constraint (concept-level, verbal)

nodes.label: 3.3.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Construct a competitive benchmark by imposing P = MK and computing P*, Q*, TS (concept-level + application; the §3.3.1 novelty is the comparison, not the components)

nodes.label: 3.3.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Distinguish surplus transfer (CS→PS) from surplus destruction (DWL) verbally (concept-level, the 'where did the surplus go?' narrative)

nodes.label: 3.3.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Apply the MO=MK procedure twice (once per market segment) and sum the resulting profits (A-domain candidate; a 'two-market monopoly' variant)

nodes.label: 3.3.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compare winst with vs without price discrimination and quantify the extra PS captured (reken skill)

nodes.label: 3.3.2 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Name and verify the three operational conditions for price discrimination: separable segments, no resale, different elasticities (concept-level; partly covered by D24 but worth a procedure check)

nodes.label: 3.3.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Characterise monopolistische concurrentie (many sellers, productdifferentiatie, free entry, limited short-run price-setting power) (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.3.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Characterise oligopolie (few sellers, strategic interaction, high entry barriers, price leadership / collusion risk) (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.3.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Classify a given real-world market into one of the four structures using the characteristic table (concept-level application)

nodes.label: 3.3.3 missing unit flag 4
nodes.flag: Explain the incentive to collude in oligopolie and why cartels are illegal + unstable (concept-level; hooks into the §3.3.3 Gap 1 revision plan)

nodes.label: 3.4.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Verbal market-failure framing: who bears the ignored cost, and why does the market price undercount it (concept-level)

nodes.label: 3.4.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: State the Coase theorem condition (low transaction costs, clear property rights) and argue when private negotiation suffices (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 3.4.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Evaluate the patent trade-off: innovation incentive vs monopoly welfare loss (concept-level + verbal; the §3.4.3↔§3.2 integration)

nodes.label: 3.4.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Enumerate policy instruments for negative externalities with a real example each (concept-level, breadth)

nodes.label: 4.3.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Define absolute advantage (lower input requirement for the same output) vs comparative advantage (lower opportunity cost) and argue why CA — not AA — drives trade (concept-level, mastery_target understand; A18 covers the calculation but not the conceptual distinction cleanly)

nodes.label: 4.3.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Refute the 'better at everything so no trade' misconception using an opportunity-cost comparison (concept-level + verbal; the 'aha moment' exam skill)

nodes.label: 4.3.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute pre-trade and post-specialisation production for each country and quantify the world-output gain (A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 4.3.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Define terms-of-trade as the exchange ratio between two goods and compare consumption bundles before vs after trade (concept-level + reken)

nodes.label: 4.3.2 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Determine the mutually-beneficial range of terms-of-trade from the two countries' opportunity costs (concept-level; the bounded-interval reasoning)

nodes.label: 4.3.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute imports = domestic Qd − domestic Qa at a given world price (A-domain candidate; the free-trade baseline calculation)

nodes.label: 4.3.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Critically evaluate 'tariffs protect domestic jobs' using winners-vs-losers surplus accounting (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling)

nodes.label: 4.4.1 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute real wage = nominal wage / CPI × 100 and interpret the direction (A-domain candidate; I09 is close but macro-framed)

nodes.label: 4.4.1 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Explain why economists prefer real values (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 4.4.2 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Define waardevast (indexed to inflation) vs welvaartsvast (indexed to wage growth) vs bevroren (no indexering) — pensioen-indexering vocab (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

nodes.label: 4.4.2 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compute the new pension under each of the three indexering regimes and compute the real purchasing-power loss under bevroren (A-domain candidate)

nodes.label: 4.4.2 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Evaluate the pensioner-vs-fund trade-off across the three regimes (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling prep)

nodes.label: 4.4.2 missing unit flag 4
nodes.flag: Apply the purchasing-power logic under deflation (concept-level; reversed-sign case, good diagnostic)

nodes.label: 4.4.3 missing unit flag 1
nodes.flag: Compute real interest rate ≈ nominal − inflation and interpret purchasing-power effect on savings (concept-level + reken; partial overlap with H15 which is macro-framed)

nodes.label: 4.4.3 missing unit flag 2
nodes.flag: Compute real GDP growth from nominal GDP change and GDP deflator change (A-domain candidate; the deflator-adjustment mechanic)

nodes.label: 4.4.3 missing unit flag 3
nodes.flag: Explain why central banks target ~2% inflation, connecting real interest rate, purchasing power, and wage-earners (concept-level + verbal; exam integration question — the §4.4.3 payoff)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Draw a (P,Q) graph from a table with the economist's axis convention (P vertical, Q horizontal) (A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Read values from a (P,Q) graph; interpolate between data points (A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Classify a normal vs inferior good response to an income change (concept-level, prerequisite for D11 inkomenselasticiteit)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Horizontal sum: aggregate individual demand tables into collective demand (A-domain candidate, mirror of A31 for aanbod)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Algebraic horizontal sum of linear demand functions (A-domain candidate, Q-at-equal-P)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Recognise the kink in collective demand when one consumer exits (concept-level)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Draw an upward-sloping supply curve with correct economist axes (A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute GVK = TVK/Q and GCK = TCK/Q (A-domain candidates; A14 covers GTK but not GVK/GCK separately)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why GCK falls as Q rises (spreading fixed costs) (concept-level, foundation for long-run equilibrium in §3.1.3)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Observe GO = P at a constant price (concept-level, a single-step recognition)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Determine surplus (aanbodoverschot) vs shortage (vraagoverschot) at a non-equilibrium price and calculate its size (A-domain candidate, prerequisite for government price interventions in §2.4)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Reason about simultaneous supply+demand shifts: determinate Q-direction but ambiguous P-direction (concept-level, diagnostic checkpoint)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute MK and MO from a table by taking differences (table-method, precedes A11/A12/A13 differentiation — the pre-calculus foundation sequence must NOT use calculus; a table-only variant should be its own unit)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Observe MK constant for linear TK vs increasing for quadratic TK (concept-level pattern recognition)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Observe MO = P when price is constant (concept-level)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Approximate MK from TK = a + bQ² as 2bQ without formal differentiation (early pre-calculus bridge; a distinct unit from A11 'Afgeleide bepalen')
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Verify a profit optimum by checking winst at Q−1, Q, Q+1 (A-domain candidate, good-habits-building step)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Classify demand as elastic vs inelastic from |Ev| against the 1-threshold (concept-level, tight pairing with A15)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain intuitively why substitute-rich or luxury goods are more elastic (concept-level; necessity-vs-luxury, substitute availability)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: State and apply the Ev↔omzet-direction rule to advise a pricing move (concept-level, the business-relevance payoff of §2.1.1)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Classify normal vs inferior from Ei sign; luxury vs necessity from |Ei| vs 1 (concept-level, pairs with A17)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Read a multi-variable demand function Qx(Px, Py, Y) and evaluate at a point (A-domain candidate, ceteris paribus in action — the Part-C novel skill)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Predict Qd change from elasticity × percentage change in driver (A-domain candidate; inverts the measurement formula)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Verbal: interpret CS as aggregate willingness-to-pay minus paid-price (concept-level, welfare-framing)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Reinterpret the supply curve as the marginal cost curve (concept-level; critical for monopoly welfare in §3.3.1)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute total surplus TS = CS + PS and recognise it as the competitive-equilibrium welfare metric (concept-level)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Determine the short-side rule (transactions at a forced price = min(Qd, Qs)) and name which side rations (concept-level + application)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Give a verbal Pareto-efficiency definition and argue why competitive equilibrium satisfies it (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Distinguish consumer price Pc vs producer price Pp and the tax wedge = Pc − Pp (concept-level + grafisch)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute afwentelingspercentage = (Pc − P*) / tax × 100 and interpret it (A-domain candidate; the consumer-burden metric)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Apply the surplus-accounting identity: old TS = new CS + new PS + tax revenue + DWL (concept-level, completeness check)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain that subsidies cause DWL via overproduction beyond the Pareto-efficient quantity (concept-level, counterintuitive to students)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute government subsidy expenditure = subsidy × Q_new (reken skill, A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Structure a beoordeel-answer: who wins, who loses under a max price — renters-with-a-unit vs renters-without (concept-level + verbal skill)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute the cost to the government of buying up the aanbodoverschot = (Qs − Qd) × Pmin (reken skill, A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Evaluate distributional effect: who gains (producers), who pays (consumers + taxpayers) (concept-level + verbal)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Invert the demand function to read price from a given quota quantity (A-domain candidate; bridges to §3.2 monopoly mechanics)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compare welfare effects of quota vs minimum price vs tax for the same producer-price target (concept-level + analytical; exam-integration skill)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why producers can prefer a quota to free-market output (concept-level, income-certainty argument)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Name the four characteristics of volkomen concurrentie and recognise them in a concrete market (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why an individual firm in volkomen concurrentie is a hoeveelheidsaanpasser / price taker (concept-level)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Show P = GO = MO for the individual firm (grafisch + concept; the horizontal price line representation)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Apply the power rule to differentiate aQ² + bQ + c, producing 2aQ + b (A-domain candidate; A11 covers 'Afgeleide bepalen' generally but not this specific TK→MK recipe)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: State MO = P as a special case for a price-taker firm (concept-level, tight pair with §3.1.1)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Herleid GTK uit TK door term-voor-term delen door Q (A-domain candidate; the 500/Q-term is the didactic bottleneck explicitly scaffolded into §3.1.2)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Argue that entry erodes positive profit and exit eliminates losses, so long-run profit = 0 in volkomen concurrentie (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Recognise the long-run equilibrium geometric condition P = min GTK = MK and explain why (concept-level + grafisch)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Name the typical causes of monopoly — legal, natural, patent — with an example each (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Contrast prijszetter vs hoeveelheidsaanpasser and link to the slope of the firm-level demand curve (concept-level)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Judge real-world market power claims against the four structural characteristics (concept-level + verbal analysis; the 'is Apple really a monopolist?' style question)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why MO < P for a monopolist — the 'price-cut-on-all-units' argument (concept-level, the non-obvious insight of this paragraph)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Observe that the MO curve has twice the slope of a linear demand curve (concept-level + grafisch; pattern recognition before calculus)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Build TO = P×Q from a linear P(Q) and expand into aQ + bQ² form (A-domain candidate; step-0 of the §3.2.3 procedure)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Execute the full 6-step monopoly procedure end-to-end (A35 is registered as 'Max. winst monopolist'; this procedure-execution skill is what it should canonically encode — verify A35's procedure field covers it)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why charging above P* reduces profit — the demand constraint (concept-level, verbal)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Construct a competitive benchmark by imposing P = MK and computing P*, Q*, TS (concept-level + application; the §3.3.1 novelty is the comparison, not the components)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Distinguish surplus transfer (CS→PS) from surplus destruction (DWL) verbally (concept-level, the 'where did the surplus go?' narrative)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Apply the MO=MK procedure twice (once per market segment) and sum the resulting profits (A-domain candidate; a 'two-market monopoly' variant)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compare winst with vs without price discrimination and quantify the extra PS captured (reken skill)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Name and verify the three operational conditions for price discrimination: separable segments, no resale, different elasticities (concept-level; partly covered by D24 but worth a procedure check)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Characterise monopolistische concurrentie (many sellers, productdifferentiatie, free entry, limited short-run price-setting power) (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Characterise oligopolie (few sellers, strategic interaction, high entry barriers, price leadership / collusion risk) (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Classify a given real-world market into one of the four structures using the characteristic table (concept-level application)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain the incentive to collude in oligopolie and why cartels are illegal + unstable (concept-level; hooks into the §3.3.3 Gap 1 revision plan)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Verbal market-failure framing: who bears the ignored cost, and why does the market price undercount it (concept-level)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: State the Coase theorem condition (low transaction costs, clear property rights) and argue when private negotiation suffices (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Evaluate the patent trade-off: innovation incentive vs monopoly welfare loss (concept-level + verbal; the §3.4.3↔§3.2 integration)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Enumerate policy instruments for negative externalities with a real example each (concept-level, breadth)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Define absolute advantage (lower input requirement for the same output) vs comparative advantage (lower opportunity cost) and argue why CA — not AA — drives trade (concept-level, mastery_target understand; A18 covers the calculation but not the conceptual distinction cleanly)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Refute the 'better at everything so no trade' misconception using an opportunity-cost comparison (concept-level + verbal; the 'aha moment' exam skill)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute pre-trade and post-specialisation production for each country and quantify the world-output gain (A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Define terms-of-trade as the exchange ratio between two goods and compare consumption bundles before vs after trade (concept-level + reken)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Determine the mutually-beneficial range of terms-of-trade from the two countries' opportunity costs (concept-level; the bounded-interval reasoning)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute imports = domestic Qd − domestic Qa at a given world price (A-domain candidate; the free-trade baseline calculation)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Critically evaluate 'tariffs protect domestic jobs' using winners-vs-losers surplus accounting (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute real wage = nominal wage / CPI × 100 and interpret the direction (A-domain candidate; I09 is close but macro-framed)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why economists prefer real values (concept-level, mastery_target understand)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Define waardevast (indexed to inflation) vs welvaartsvast (indexed to wage growth) vs bevroren (no indexering) — pensioen-indexering vocab (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute the new pension under each of the three indexering regimes and compute the real purchasing-power loss under bevroren (A-domain candidate)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Evaluate the pensioner-vs-fund trade-off across the three regimes (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling prep)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Apply the purchasing-power logic under deflation (concept-level; reversed-sign case, good diagnostic)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute real interest rate ≈ nominal − inflation and interpret purchasing-power effect on savings (concept-level + reken; partial overlap with H15 which is macro-framed)
Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Compute real GDP growth from nominal GDP change and GDP deflator change (A-domain candidate; the deflator-adjustment mechanic)

Owned target exercise missing-unit flag: Explain why central banks target ~2% inflation, connecting real interest rate, purchasing power, and wage-earners (concept-level + verbal; exam integration question — the §4.4.3 payoff)
